content:
CSS

Css Animation

Coding

Header

Web Development

2 min read
Just now

--

Most websites feel static. A few small animations change that instantly, without touching your JavaScript bundle size or your Lighthouse score.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Vitaly Gariev on UnsplashOver the past few weeks I’ve been building out a photography portfolio site, and most of the “wow” moments came from CSS alone: a hero section that breathes, a cursor that reacts to what you’re hovering over, cards that lift like they’re made of paper. No animation library. No extra dependencies. Just CSS.
Here are five animations I actually use in production, with the code.

1. The Breathing Hero Background
A hero section feels dead when it’s just a static gradient. Add a slow, looping scale-and-shift, and it feels like the page is alive before the user even scrolls.

2. A Custom Cursor That Reacts
A default cursor is invisible. A custom one that lags slightly behind the mouse, or grows when hovering over a clickable element, makes the whole interface feel tactile.
The CSS does the animating ,the JS just tracks position and toggles a class. Keep the transition short (under 0.2s) or the cursor feels disconnected from the actual mouse.

3. Cards That Lift on Hover
This one is tiny, but it’s the difference between a page that feels flat and one that feels layered.
will-change: transform matters here-it tells the browser to move this element to its own compositor layer ahead of time, so the hover doesn't cause a repaint stutter on lower-end devices.

4. Staggered Fade-In on Scroll
Instead of an entire section popping in at once, stagger it-each child appears slightly after the last. It reads as intentional rather than jarring.
Pair this with an IntersectionObserver in JS to add a .visible class when the section scrolls into view, and trigger the animation only then,otherwise everything fires on page load, off-screen, before the user ever sees it.

5. The Underline That Draws Itself
A simple nav link hover effect that feels far more polished than text-decoration: underline.

The Real Lesson
None of these animations are complicated. The reason they feel good isn’t clever code — it’s restraint. Short durations for anything hover-triggered (150–300ms), longer durations for anything ambient and looping (10+ seconds), and transform/opacity only, since those are the two properties the browser can animate without triggering a layout recalculation.
Add one of these to a page that currently has none, and you’ll notice the difference immediately. Add all five to the same page, and you’ll notice a different kind of difference — it starts to feel busy. Pick the ones that match what your site is actually trying to say.

Building a photography portfolio site right now and using a few of these -happy to go deeper on any of them in the comments.
